en.extract:
The chestnut-rumped thornbill (Acanthiza uropygialis) is a small passerine bird in the family Acanthizidae, endemic to Australia.
Specially named for its pale-chestnut rump from mediaeval Latin uropygium, the rump.
Its other names include chestnut-tailed or chestnut-backed thornbill, tit or tit-warbler, chestnut-rumped tit or tit-warbler.
A plain, pale thornbill, with pale eyes, it is widespread throughout inland Australia, west of the Great Dividing Range to the West Australian coast but is absent in the far north and humid southwest Western Australia. It is a cooperative breeder like some of the other thornbills 


Taxonomy and naming
Described and classified by John Gould in 1838 in Synopsis of Birds of Australia, from a specimen collected in Liverpool Plains NSW.
Type, male, No17602 (525) is in the Academy of Natural Sciences, Philadelphia, United States.  A specimen from the Gould collection is in the British Museum.
Mathews (1912) recognised a number of different subspecies of Acanthiza Uropygialis: A.u.ruthergleni, mellori, augusta, nea, murchisoni and condora,  with A.u.kycheringi added in 1922.  Campbell (1925) added subspecies Geobasilus uropygialis moora and G.u. erema.  Where the genus Geobasileus, referred to ground feeding thornbills.
However, Mack (1936) and Mayr & Serventy (1938) reorganised the species into two subspecies, A.u. uropygialis and A.u. augusta. Recent studies do not recognise subspecies but indicate that variations in plumage colouration are clinal.
Acanthiza had been previously placed in the family Pardalotidae, but are now part of the Acanthizidae.


Description
Length: 9.8 cm (9-11); wing span 15.5 cm (14-16.5); weight: 6 g.
Mid-sized thornbill similar in size and shape to inland thornbill (A. apicalis) and slaty-backed thornbill (A. robustirostris).
Pale and plain with pale iris similar to buff-rumped (Acanthiza reguloides), western (A.inornata), slender-billed (A. iredalei) and yellow-rumped thornbills (A. chrysorrhoa).
Pale grey-brown upper, rich chestnut rump and mostly black tail with pale tips, pale mottled ear-coverts and rufous suffusion on forehead and crown. Plain whitish underbody.  The sexes are alike, with no seasonal variation.
Slight geographic variations, birds of the more arid inland are paler and greyer above, with a slightly paler rump patch and cleaner white below than those in coastal and subcoastal regions. May be confused with other thornbills having a rufous-brown rump patch such as brown thornbill (A. pusilla), inland thornbill (A. apicalis) and slaty-backed thornbills (A. robustirostris).
The chestnut-rumped thornbill is gregarious, usually in small flocks, twos or threes and sometimes with other thornbills and small passerines.  Active and restless, the chestnut-rumped thornbill forages in shrubs and trees, searching briskly, flitting and hopping in foliage and low branches, probing into crevices and bark. It also hops on the ground searching among fallen debris.
Flight similar to other thornbills, cover to cover in low undulating dashes.
Voice is penetrating, with a far-carrying song of similar phrases. It also mimics other birds.
Juveniles are similar to adults, but plainer with duller head pattern, and pale buff tail tips, not white. Iris is slightly duller, cream or greyish white.  Nearly fledged juveniles have pale yellow gape.


Distribution and population
Endemic to mainland Australia.
Qld: Widespread in S, to W of Great Divide, in South Western, South Central and W. South-Eastern Regions.  Generally S of 23° S.
NSW & ACT: Widespread W of Great Divide, including w. slopes of Northern Tablelands and southern Tablelands, Canberra.
Vic: Widespread in Mallee, Wimmera and North Districts N of 37°S.
SA: Widespread in many regions, but generally absent from SE (though a few records exist).
WA: Generally absent from Nullarbor Plain.  Farther west largely absent from south coast, widespread in areas W of 123°E from 32°S North to 22°S in Pilbara region.  Widespread in Gibson Desert, and scattered sites in Great Victoria Desert.


Movements

Chestnut-rumped thornbills are resident or sedentary, but some movement occurs.  No large-scale movements are known, though some individuals occasionally move long distances. Non-breeding flocks disperse before breeding.


Habitat
They are found in dry woodlands and shrublands, mainly of mulga and malleeeucalypts. Also in thickets, saltbush, bluebush, lignum, open pastoral country, among dead trees and stumps.
They occur in arid and semi-arid zones extending to temperate and subtropical zones,
in a wide variety of land forms including sand dunes, flood plains, rocky hillsides, plateaux and gorges.


Behaviour


Feeding
Chestnut-rumped thornbills are mainly insectivores but occasionally eat seeds. Studies on stomach contents show spiders, insects, plants, seeds and buds.
They mostly forage in foliage and from branches of shrubs and low trees but also regularly on the ground, by gleaning 81.8% (leaves, twigs, branches, ground),  sallying 4.4% and 13.9% by probing into bark. The young are fed tiny insects and small white grubs.
They are gregarious and rarely seen singly. Feeding flocks are usually small parties of up to ten, and less often up to 20 birds, associating with brown thornbill (A. pusilla), yellow-rumped thornbill (A. chrysorrhoa), southern whiteface (Aphelocephala leucopsis), white-browed scrubwrens (Sericornis frontalis) or redthroats (Pyrrholaemus brunneus), and occasionally joined by silvereyes (Zosterops lateralis).
In South Australia during winter, they may congregate in mixed feeding groups with yellow (A. nana), yellow-rumped (A. chrysorrhoa) and buff-rumped thornbills (A. reguloides), with up to 100 or more birds in groups.


Breeding
Most records of chestnut-rumped thornbills breeding involve pairs; however, they appear occasionally to breed co-operatively.
Both adults are involved with building nests,  which are small, neat and domed usually ovoid, with a rounded entrance at the side or near the top. If inside a hollow, the entrance is often flush with the opening of the hollow. Materials used are dried grass, bark strips, plant stems, moss, lichen and spider web, with a lining of feathers, wool or fur.
Nest sites are usually in hollows, often vertical, in live and dead trees, with the entrance at the end of a spout, crevice or knothole, in the side of a branch or trunk. They also nest in stumps, logs or branches lying on the ground and in fence-posts.
The breeding season is June to December, and two broods, possibly three can be raised per season. The ovoid eggs are laid at two-day intervals. They are very fine, slightly glossy, flesh-white and minutely freckled all over with reddish brown and purplish brown, particularly at the large-end.  
The chicks are altricial and nidicolous, with both adults feeding the nestlings and fledglings. The fledging period is around 18–20 days.
Chestnut-rumped thornbills are known to be parasitised by Horsfield's bronze cuckoos (Chalcites basalis).


Conservation
Range: The chestnut-rumped thornbill has an extremely large range, and therefore does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence <20,000 km2 combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation).
Population trend: Though the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (>30% decline over ten years or three generations).
Population size:  This has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (<10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be >10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure).
For these reasons the species is evaluated as least concern.
However, the chestnut-rumped thornbill is on a list of declining woodland birds, characterized mostly by being ground or low-shrub feeders and dwellers as well as being predominantly insectivores. The clearing of woodlands on fertile soils and overgrazing of remaining native vegetation, has resulted in fewer ground-dwelling invertebrate prey, causing chestnut-rumped thornbill's range to be possibly contracting towards the inland.


Gallery


References


External links

Photos, Videos and Sounds

Graeme Chapman image of chestnut-rumped thornbill
Greg Oakley image of chestnut-rumped thornbill
Videos, Photos and sounds on the Internet Bird Collection
Sound recording of chestnut-rumped thornbill on Cornell Laboratory of Ornithology's Macaulay Library website

[[File:Chestnut-rumped Thornbills, posturing.jpg|thumb|Chestnut-rumped thornbills posturing, (sequence of 5 photos in less than 2 seconds, Sturt Desert, NSW).]]
The '''chestnut-rumped thornbill''' ('''''Acanthiza uropygialis''''') is a small passerine [[bird]] in the family [[Acanthizidae]], [[Endemism|endemic]] to Australia.

Specially named for its pale-chestnut rump from mediaeval Latin ''uropygium'', the rump.<ref name="Higgins">Higgins, P. J., & Peter, J. M. (Eds.). (2002). ''Handbook of Australian, New Zealand and Antarctic Birds'' (Vol. 6, pp. 468-478). Melbourne: Oxford University Press.</ref>
Its other names include chestnut-tailed or chestnut-backed thornbill, tit or tit-warbler, chestnut-rumped tit or tit-warbler.<ref name="Higgins"/>
A plain, pale thornbill, with pale eyes, it is widespread throughout inland Australia, west of the [[Great Dividing Range]] to the West Australian coast but is absent in the far north and humid southwest [[Western Australia]].<ref name="Pizzey">Pizzey, G., & Knight, F. (2012). ''The field guide to the birds of Australia'' (S. Pizzey Ed. 9 ed.). Sydney: Harper Collins.</ref> It is a [[cooperative breeding|cooperative breeder]] like some of the other thornbills <ref name="Nicholls">Nicholls, J. A., Double, M. C., Rowell, D. M., & Magrath, R. D. (2000). The evolution of cooperative and pair breeding in thornbills Acanthiza (Pardalotidae). ''Journal of Avian Biology, 31'', 165-176. [http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1034/j.1600-048X.2000.310208.x/abstract doi: 10.1034/j.1600-048X.2000.310208.x]</ref>

==Taxonomy and naming==
Described and classified by [[John Gould]] in 1838 in ''Synopsis of Birds of Australia'', from a specimen collected in Liverpool Plains NSW.<ref name="GouldH">Gould, J. (1865). ''Handbook to the birds of Australia''. London: Published by the author. [http://nla.gov.au/nla.aus-f4773-3-s119-e-cd] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161207021954/http://nla.gov.au/nla.aus-f4773-3-s119-e-cd |date=2016-12-07 }}</ref>

Type, male, [http://www.ansp.org/~/media/Files/ans/research/sys-ornithology/ANSP_typelist_v1.ashx No17602 (525)] is in the [http://www.ansp.org/  Academy of Natural Sciences, Philadelphia], United States.  A specimen from the Gould collection is in the [[British Museum]].<ref name="campbell25"/>

[[Gregory Mathews|Mathews]]<ref name="Mathews">Mathews, G. M. (1912). A List to the Birds of Australia. ''Novitates Zoologicae, 18'', 171-455. [https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/22250#page/374/mode/1up]</ref> (1912) recognised a number of different subspecies of ''Acanthiza Uropygialis: A.u.ruthergleni, mellori, augusta, nea, murchisoni'' and ''condora'',<ref name="Mathews"/>  with ''A.u.kycheringi'' added in 1922.  Campbell<ref name="campbell25">Campbell, A. G., & Kilsyth, J. P. (1925). Thornbills of the Genus Geobasileus. ''Emu, 25''(2), 57-68. [http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU925057.htm doi:10.1071/MU925057]</ref> (1925) added subspecies ''Geobasilus uropygialis moora'' and ''G.u. erema''.  Where the genus ''Geobasileus'', referred to ground feeding thornbills.<ref name="campbell22">Campbell, A. G. (1922). Acanthiza or Thornbills. ''Emu, 22''(3), 192-199. [http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU922192.htm doi :10.1071/MU922192]</ref>

However, [[George Mack (ornithologist)|Mack]]<ref name="Mack">Mack, G. (1936). A systematic revision of the Australian thornbills. ''Memoirs of Museum Victoria, 10'', 86-118. retrieved 23 Oct 2013 from [https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/120023#page/90/mode/1up https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/120023#page/90/mode/1up]</ref> (1936) and [[Ernst Mayr|Mayr]] & [[Dominic Serventy|Serventy]]<ref name="mayr">Mayr, E., & Serventy, D. L. (1938). A Review of the Genus Acanthiza Vigors and Horsfield. ''Emu, 38''(3), 244-292. [http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU938245.htm doi:10.1071/MU938245]</ref> (1938) reorganised the species into two subspecies, ''A.u. uropygialis'' and ''A.u. augusta''. Recent studies do not recognise subspecies but indicate that variations in plumage colouration are clinal.<ref name="Higgins"/><ref name="schodde">Schodde, R., & Mason, I. J. (1999). ''The Directory of Australian Birds: Passerines''. Melbourne: CSIRO Publishing.</ref><ref name="Christidis">Christidis, L., & Boles, W. E. (2008). ''Systematics and taxonomy of Australian birds''. Melbourne: CSIRO Publishing.</ref>

''[[Acanthiza]]'' had been previously placed in the family [[Pardalotidae]], but are now part of the [[Acanthizidae]].<ref name="Christidis"/>

[[File:Chestnut-rumped Thornbill, on branch.jpg|thumb|left|Chestnut-rumped thornbill (Sturt Desert, NSW).]]
==Description==

Length: 9.8 cm (9-11); wing span 15.5 cm (14-16.5); weight: 6 g.<ref name="Higgins"/><ref name="Johnstone"/>

Mid-sized thornbill similar in size and shape to [[inland thornbill]] (''A. apicalis'') and [[slaty-backed thornbill]] (''A. robustirostris'').
Pale and plain with pale iris similar to [[buff-rumped thornbill|buff-rumped]] (''Acanthiza reguloides''), [[western thornbill|western]] (''A.inornata''), [[slender-billed thornbill|slender-billed]] (''A. iredalei'') and [[yellow-rumped thornbill]]s (''A. chrysorrhoa'').<ref name="Higgins"/>

Pale grey-brown upper, rich chestnut rump and mostly black tail with pale tips, pale mottled ear-coverts and rufous suffusion on forehead and crown. Plain whitish underbody.  The sexes are alike, with no seasonal variation.<ref name="Higgins"/><ref name="Pizzey"/>

Slight geographic variations, birds of the more arid inland are paler and greyer above, with a slightly paler rump patch and cleaner white below than those in coastal and subcoastal regions. May be confused with other thornbills having a rufous-brown rump patch such as [[brown thornbill]] (''A. pusilla''), [[inland thornbill]] (''A. apicalis'') and [[slaty-backed thornbill]]s (''A. robustirostris'').<ref name="Higgins"/><ref name="Sedgwick"/><ref name="FordParker"/>

The chestnut-rumped thornbill is gregarious, usually in small flocks, twos or threes and sometimes with other thornbills and small passerines.  Active and restless, the chestnut-rumped thornbill forages in shrubs and trees, searching briskly, flitting and hopping in foliage and low branches, probing into crevices and bark. It also hops on the ground searching among fallen debris.<ref name="Higgins"/><ref name="Sedgwick"/>

Flight similar to other thornbills, cover to cover in low undulating dashes.<ref name="Higgins"/>

Voice is penetrating, with a far-carrying song of similar phrases. It also mimics other birds.<ref name="Pizzey"/>

Juveniles are similar to adults, but plainer with duller head pattern, and pale buff tail tips, not white. Iris is slightly duller, cream or greyish white.  Nearly fledged juveniles have pale yellow gape.<ref name="Higgins"/><ref name="Johnstone"/>

==Distribution and population==

Endemic to mainland Australia.

[[Queensland|'''Qld:''']] Widespread in S, to W of [[Great Dividing Range|Great Divide]], in South Western, South Central and W. South-Eastern Regions.  Generally S of 23° S.<ref name="FordParker">Ford, J., & Parker, S. A. (1973). First record of Acanthiza robustirostris in Queensland. ''Emu, 73''(1), 27 - 27. [http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU973027b.htm doi:10.1071/MU973027b]</ref><ref name="Ford">Ford, J., Greensmith, A., & Reid, N. (1980). Notes on the distribution of Queensland birds. ''Sunbird: Journal of the Queensland Ornithological Society'', 11(3/4), 58-70. [http://search.informit.com.au/documentSummary;dn=888521476186616;res=IELHSS].</ref>

[[New South Wales|'''NSW & ACT:''']] Widespread W of Great Divide, including w. slopes of Northern Tablelands and southern Tablelands, [[Canberra]].<ref name="Higgins"/><ref name="Hobbs">Hobbs, J. N. (1961). The birds of south-west New South Wales. ''Emu, 61''(1), 21 - 55. [http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU961021.htm doi:10.1071/MU961021]</ref>

[[Victoria (Australia)|'''Vic:''']] Widespread in [[Mallee (Victoria)|Mallee]], [[Wimmera]] and North Districts N of 37°S.<ref name="Higgins"/><ref name="Chisholm">Chisholm, A. H. (1935). Birds of the Mallee National Park. ''Emu, 35''(2), 133 - 136. [http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU935133.htm doi:10.1071/MU935133]</ref>

[[South Australia|'''SA:''']] Widespread in many regions, but generally absent from SE (though a few records exist).<ref name="Higgins"/><ref name="Ashton">Ashton, C. B. (1985). The birds of the Aldinga-Sellicks Beach scrub. ''South Australian Ornithology'', 29(7), 169-179.</ref><ref name="Badman79">Badman, F. J. (1979). Birds of the southern and western Lake Eyre drainage. ''South Australian Ornithology, 28''(3), 57-81.</ref>

[[Western Australia|'''WA:''']] Generally absent from [[Nullarbor Plain]].  Farther west largely absent from south coast, widespread in areas W of 123°E from 32°S North to 22°S in [[Pilbara]] region.  Widespread in [[Gibson Desert]], and scattered sites in [[Great Victoria Desert]].<ref name="Higgins"/><ref name="Parker">Parker, S. (1969). New and interesting distribution records of central Australian birds. ''South Australian Ornithology, 25''(3), 59-71.</ref><ref name="Sedgwick49">Sedgwick, E. H. (1949). Mixed associations of small birds in the south-west of Western Australia. ''Emu, 49''(1), 9 - 13. [http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU949009.htm doi:10.1071/MU949009]</ref>

===Movements===
[[File:Chestnut-rumped Thornbill, take-off.jpg|thumb|right|Chestnut-rumped thornbill, about to fly off (Sturt Desert, NSW).]]
[[File:Chestnut-rumped Thornbill, tail detail.jpg|thumb|Chestnut-rumped thornbill, rear flight view (Sturt Desert, NSW).]]

Chestnut-rumped thornbills are resident or sedentary,<ref name="Brookeretal">Brooker, M. G., Ridpath, M. G., Estbergs, J. A., Bywater, J., .Hart, D. S., & Jones, M. S. (1979). Bird observations on the North-western Nullarbor Plain and neighbouring regions, 1967-1978. ''Emu, 79''(4), 176 - 190. [http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU9790176.htm]</ref> but some movement occurs.  No large-scale movements are known, though some individuals occasionally move long distances.<ref name="Baldwin">Baldwin, M. (1975). Birds of Inverell District, NSW. ''Emu, 75''(3), 113 - 120. [http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU9750113.htm]</ref> Non-breeding flocks disperse before breeding.<ref name="Higgins"/><ref name="McEvey">McEvey, A. R., & Middleton, W. G. (1968). Birds and vegetation between Perth and Adelaide (Results of the Harold Hall Australian Expedition, No.12). ''Emu, 68''(3), 161 - 212. [http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU953319.htm]</ref>

===Habitat===
They are found in dry woodlands and shrublands, mainly of [[Acacia aneura|mulga]] and [[Mallee Woodlands and Shrublands|mallee]]<ref name="Higgins"/><ref name="Rix">Rix, C. E. (1943). A Review of the birds between the Mt. Lofty Ranges and the River Murray - a site for a real sanctuary. ''The South Australian Ornithologist, 16''(6-8), 57-78. [http://www.birdssa.asn.au/images/saopdfs/Volume16/1943V16P057.pdf]</ref>[[eucalypts]].<ref name="Hobbs"/> Also in thickets, [[saltbush]], [[Maireana|bluebush]], [[Muehlenbeckia florulenta|lignum]], open pastoral country, among dead trees and stumps.<ref name="Pizzey"/>
They occur in arid and semi-arid zones extending to temperate and subtropical zones,<ref name="Higgins"/><ref name="ALA"/><ref name="BLI"/>
in a wide variety of land forms including sand dunes, flood plains, rocky hillsides, plateaux and gorges.<ref name="Higgins"/><ref name="FordSedgwick">Ford, J., & Sedgwick, E. H. (1967). Bird distribution in the Nullarbor Plain and Great Victoria Desert region, Western Australia. ''Emu, 67''(2), 99 - 124. [http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU967099.htm doi:10.1071/MU967099]</ref><ref name="Gee">Gee, P., Gee, I., & Read, J. (1996). An annotated bird list from the Davenport Range, South Australia. ''South Australian Ornithology'', 32(4/5), 76-81.</ref>

==Behaviour==

===Feeding===

Chestnut-rumped thornbills are mainly insectivores but occasionally eat seeds. Studies on stomach contents show spiders, insects, plants, seeds and buds.<ref name="Higgins"/>

They mostly forage in foliage and from branches of shrubs and low trees but also regularly on the ground, by gleaning 81.8% (leaves, twigs, branches, ground),  sallying 4.4% and 13.9% by probing into bark.<ref name="Sedgwick"/> The young are fed tiny insects and small white grubs.<ref>Leach, H. A. C. (1928 ). The birds of Central Northern Victoria. ''Emu, 28''(2), 83 - 99. [http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU928083.htm doi:10.1071/MU928083]</ref>

They are gregarious and rarely seen singly. Feeding flocks are usually small parties of up to ten, and less often up to 20 birds, associating with [[brown thornbill]] (''A. pusilla''),<ref name="FordSedgwick"/> [[yellow-rumped thornbill]] (''A. chrysorrhoa''), [[southern whiteface]] (''Aphelocephala leucopsis''), [[white-browed scrubwren]]s (''Sericornis frontalis'') or [[redthroat]]s (''Pyrrholaemus brunneus''), and occasionally joined by silvereyes (''Zosterops lateralis'').<ref name="Higgins"/>

In [[South Australia]] during winter, they may congregate in mixed feeding groups with [[yellow thornbill|yellow]] (''A. nana''), [[yellow-rumped thornbill|yellow-rumped]] (''A. chrysorrhoa'') and [[buff-rumped thornbill]]s (''A. reguloides''), with up to 100 or more birds in groups.<ref name="Higgins"/>

===Breeding===

Most records of chestnut-rumped thornbills breeding involve pairs; however, they appear occasionally to [[cooperative breeding|breed co-operatively]].<ref name="Nicholls"/>
Both adults are involved with building nests,<ref name="Sedgwick"/>  which are small, neat and domed usually ovoid, with a rounded entrance at the side or near the top. If inside a hollow, the entrance is often flush with the opening of the hollow. Materials used are dried grass, bark strips, plant stems, moss, lichen and spider web, with a lining of feathers, wool or fur.<ref name="Higgins"/><ref name="White"/><ref name="McGilp"/>

Nest sites are usually in hollows, often vertical, in live and dead trees, with the entrance at the end of a spout, crevice or knothole, in the side of a branch or trunk. They also nest in stumps, logs or branches lying on the ground and in fence-posts.<ref name="Higgins"/><ref name="McGilp">J.N.McGilp. (1922). Birds of the Lake Frome District, South Australia. Part II. ''Emu, 22''(4), 274 - 287. [http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU922274.htm doi:10.1071/MU922274]</ref>

The breeding season is June to December, and two broods, possibly three can be raised per season. The ovoid eggs are laid at two-day intervals. They are very fine, slightly glossy, flesh-white and minutely freckled all over with reddish brown and purplish brown, particularly at the large-end.<ref name="Higgins"/><ref name="Johnstone">Johnstone, R., & Storr, G. M. (2004). ''Handbook of Western Australian Birds'' (Vol. II). Perth: Western Australian Museum.</ref><ref name="White">White, H. L. (1915). Descriptions of nests and eggs new to science. ''Emu, 15''(1), 35 - 36. [http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU915035.htm doi:10.1071/MU915035]</ref>  

The chicks are [[altricial]] and [[nidicolous]], with both adults feeding the nestlings and fledglings.<ref name="Sedgwick">Sedgwick, E. H. (1936). Notes on the Chestnut-tailed Thornbill. ''Emu, 35''(3), 231 - 232. [http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU935231.htm doi:10.1071/MU935231]</ref><ref name="Lloyd">Lloyd, A. (1982). Communal feeding in Chestnut-Rumped Thornbills. ''Sunbird: Journal of the Queensland Ornithological Society'', 12(2/3). [http://search.informit.com.au/documentSummary;dn=823883698891920;res=IELHSS]</ref> The fledging period is around 18–20 days.<ref name="Higgins"/>

Chestnut-rumped thornbills are known to be parasitised by [[Horsfield's bronze cuckoo]]s (''Chalcites basalis'').<ref name="Brooker">Brooker, M., & Brooker, L. (2003). Brood parasitism by Horsfield's Bronze-Cuckoo in a fragmented agricultural landscape in [[Western Australia]]. ''Emu, 103''(4), 357 - 361. [http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU02034.htm doi:10.1071/MU02034]</ref>

==Conservation==

'''Range:''' The chestnut-rumped thornbill has an extremely large range, and therefore does not approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the range size criterion (Extent of Occurrence <20,000 km2 combined with a declining or fluctuating range size, habitat extent/quality, or population size and a small number of locations or severe fragmentation).<ref name="BLI">Birdlife International. (2013). Species factsheet: Acanthiza uropygialis. Retrieved 14 Oct 2013, from [http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/speciesfactsheet.php?id=5435 http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/speciesfactsheet.php?id=5435]</ref>
 
'''Population trend:''' Though the population trend appears to be decreasing, the decline is not believed to be sufficiently rapid to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population trend criterion (>30% decline over ten years or three generations).<ref name="BLI"/>

'''Population size:'''  This has not been quantified, but it is not believed to approach the thresholds for Vulnerable under the population size criterion (<10,000 mature individuals with a continuing decline estimated to be >10% in ten years or three generations, or with a specified population structure).<ref name="BLI"/>

For these reasons the species is evaluated as [[least concern]].<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" />

However, the chestnut-rumped thornbill is on a list of declining woodland birds,<ref name="Watson">Watson, D. (2011). A productivity-based explanation for woodland bird declines: poorer soils yield less food. ''Emu, 111''(1), 10 -18. [http://www.publish.csiro.au/paper/MU09109.htm doi: 10.1071/MU09109]</ref><ref name="Reid">Reid, J. R. W. (1999). Threatened and declining birds in the New South Wales sheep-wheat belt: 1. Diagnosis, characteristics and management. Consultancy report to NSW National Parks and Wildlife Service.  CSIRO Wildlife and Ecology, Canberra. [http://www.environment.nsw.gov.au/resources/nature/WEDecliningBirdReport99Reid.pdf]</ref> characterized mostly by being ground or low-shrub feeders and dwellers as well as being predominantly insectivores.<ref name="Reid"/> The clearing of woodlands on fertile soils and [[overgrazing]] of remaining native vegetation, has resulted in fewer ground-dwelling invertebrate prey,<ref name="Watson"/> causing chestnut-rumped thornbill's range to be possibly contracting towards the inland.<ref name="Reid"/>
